articles.headline: The Sweet Spot for Lifting Just Got Clearer. A 30-Year Study Found It.
articles.body: How much lifting is enough to actually extend your life? A massive new study tracking more than 147,000 adults for up to three decades finally has a solid answer. Researchers found that people doing roughly 90 to 120 minutes of strength training per week had the lowest risk of dying overall. The benefits got even bigger when that lifting was paired with regular aerobic exercise. And the cancer numbers were striking too: even just a few minutes of resistance work per week was linked to meaningfully lower cancer mortality risk. More isn't always more, but some is clearly a lot better than none.
articles.takeaway: Aim for two strength sessions a week totaling 90 to 120 minutes, and pair them with cardio. That combination appears to offer the greatest longevity benefit based on three decades of data.

articles.headline: You're Probably Eating Enough Fruit. You're Still Not Getting What Your Heart Needs.
articles.body: Eating your five-a-day doesn't automatically mean your heart is covered. A major new study from researchers at Harvard, the University of Reading, and UC Davis tracked more than 30,000 people in the UK and US using actual biomarker measurements, not just diet surveys. The finding: fewer than one in five people hit the 500mg daily flavanol intake that prior research linked to a significantly lower risk of dying from cardiovascular disease. That includes people who regularly ate their recommended fruit and vegetable servings. The problem isn't quantity. It's which fruits and vegetables you're choosing.
articles.takeaway: Load up on flavanol-rich foods like blueberries, blackberries, plums, cherries, and green tea. These specific choices, not just total produce volume, appear to drive the heart-protective effect.
articles.source: Food and Function (University of Reading / Harvard Medical School)

articles.topic: daily brain training resilience anxiety depression
articles.headline: Five Minutes a Day Built a Real Mental Shield. A New Trial Just Showed How.
articles.body: Most mental health tools kick in after you're already struggling. A new study from the Center for BrainHealth at UT Dallas flips that model. Researchers put 370 adults through a digital strategy-based brain training program, with sessions as short as five minutes a day over six months. The result: every participant, whether they had a history of mental illness or not, showed measurable improvements in resilience, quality of life, and reduction in symptoms of depression, anxiety, and stress. Healthy adults also saw a boost in executive function. The takeaway isn't that an app replaces therapy. It's that proactive, daily mental reps work.
articles.takeaway: Short daily cognitive training, even just five minutes of focused strategy-based practice, can meaningfully reduce psychological distress over time. You don't have to wait for a crisis to train your brain.

articles.topic: workout timing circadian rhythm performance metabolism
articles.headline: When You Train Matters. Your Body's Internal Clock Is Paying Attention.
articles.body: The science of workout timing just got a rigorous new look. A June 2026 review published in Life Metabolism breaks down how your body's circadian clock actively governs exercise capacity, fuel metabolism, and even mitochondrial growth. Performance in both endurance and power events is time-of-day dependent, and the underlying mechanisms now include how the clock coordinates tissue-specific responses throughout the day. Your hormones, core temperature, and chronotype all factor in. The research doesn't hand you a perfect workout hour, but it makes clear: timing your training to match your biology is a real lever worth pulling.
articles.takeaway: Pay attention to when you feel strongest and most alert consistently, that window likely aligns with your circadian peak for performance. Training at the same time each day can also reinforce your body's internal rhythms.

quote.text: It never gets easier. You just get stronger.
quote.author: Kelly Slater
quote.context: Eleven-time world surfing champion, widely regarded as the greatest competitive surfer of all time.

workout.title: Pull Strong: Row, Hang and Brace in 30
workout.tip: If you don't have a pull-up bar, a sturdy door frame pull-up bar works fine. For the dead hang, focus on active shoulders, squeeze your shoulder blades down before you hang. That protects your joints and builds the right muscles.
workout.warmup: Spend 3 minutes loosening up your upper body: 10 arm circles each direction, 10 band pull-aparts or towel pull-aparts, and 30 seconds of hanging from the bar in a passive dead hang to open up the lats and shoulders.

exercises.name: Dead Hang
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with both hands shoulder-width apart. Let your shoulders pack down and back, not shrug up to your ears. Build grip and shoulder stability. Rest 60 seconds between sets.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent-Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips to about 45 degrees, keep your lower back flat. Drive the elbow straight back and squeeze at the top. Avoid rotating your torso. Go heavier than feels comfortable.

exercises.name: Resistance Band Face Pull
exercises.notes: Anchor the band at face height. Pull the band toward your nose, driving elbows out wide and up. Pause briefly at the end range. This is the most underrated shoulder health exercise you aren't doing enough.

exercises.name: Inverted Row (or Assisted Pull-Up with Band)
exercises.notes: For inverted row: use a table edge or low bar, heels on the floor, body in a plank. Pull your chest to the bar. For band-assisted pull-up: loop band on bar, place one knee in band, pull chin over bar. Both count.

exercises.name: Plank to Alternating Shoulder Tap
exercises.notes: Keep your hips square and still as you lift each hand off the floor to tap the opposite shoulder. Slow and controlled beats fast and sloppy every time. Great anti-rotation core work.

workout.cooldown: Finish with 60 seconds of passive hanging from the bar to decompress the spine, followed by a 60-second doorway chest and shoulder stretch to rebalance after all the pulling.

product.name: Gymnastic Rings - Double Circle Wood
product.blurb: Chris trains on rings 2x per week. The most effective upper body tool that fits in a bag.

meal.title: Seared Duck Breast with Roasted Asparagus and White Bean Mash
ingredients: 1 can white beans, drained and rinsed
Salt and black pepper to taste
meal.instructions: Score the duck skin in a crosshatch pattern, season generously with salt and pepper. Place duck breasts skin-side down in a cold pan, then bring to medium heat and cook 7-8 minutes until skin is crispy and golden. Flip and cook another 4-5 minutes for medium. Rest for 5 minutes before slicing. While duck rests, toss asparagus in olive oil, salt, and pepper and roast at 425F for 10-12 minutes or cook in a hot grill pan. Warm white beans in a small pan with garlic, a splash of olive oil, lemon zest, and a squeeze of lemon juice. Mash roughly with a fork, leaving some texture. Plate the white bean mash, top with sliced duck, add asparagus on the side, and finish with a drizzle of Dijon-lemon glaze made by whisking the Dijon with remaining lemon juice.
meal.tip: Starting duck skin-side down in a cold pan is the key move. It renders the fat out slowly and gives you restaurant-quality crispy skin without burning. Don't rush this step.

product.name: Greater Goods Digital Food Scale
product.blurb: Measure your food for two weeks and you will understand your body better than any diet book.
